title: Quantum-enhanced sensing from the interplay of long-range interactions and non-Hermiticity
authors: Keshav Das Agarwal, Tanoy Kanti Konar, Leela Ganesh Chandra Lakkaraju, Aditi Sen De
abstract: Long-range (LR) quantum spin systems offer promising advantages for quantum information processing and sensing. Here, we investigate parameter estimation in an long-range XX spin model coupled to a reservoir, which gives rise to an effective long-range RT-symmetric non-Hermitian iXY Hamiltonian. The interactions extend up to a tunable coordination range and decay algebraically with distance, enabling a direct comparison between long-range and short-range (SR) regimes. Focusing on the estimation of the transverse magnetic field and anisotropy parameter, we initialize the system in a fully polarized state and analyze the resulting dynamical quantum Fisher information (QFI). We show that, with suitable tuning of the system parameters, both the time and system-size scaling of the QFI are enhanced in the LR regime relative to their SR counterparts. Moreover, the non-Hermitian LR model can exhibit superior dynamical QFI compared with the corresponding Hermitian model, demonstrating a genuine metrological advantage induced by the interplay of long-range interactions and non-Hermitian effects. In contrast, we establish a no-go result at the critical magnetic field: when the probe is prepared in the lowest-energy eigenstate, the QFI scaling remains identical for the Hermitian and non-Hermitian cases.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the combination of long-range interactions and non-Hermiticity can significantly boost the sensitivity of quantum sensors during the transient evolution of a spin system. While it provides a clear advantage for dynamical sensing, the authors establish that this benefit does not extend to the estimation of parameters in stationary states.
main_problem: Investigating how the interplay between long-range interactions and non-Hermiticity can enhance quantum sensing performance, specifically for parameter estimation of magnetic fields and anisotropy.
main_result: The study finds that long-range interactions and non-Hermiticity synergistically enhance the dynamical Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) in the transient regime, though no genuine advantage is found for stationary-state sensing.
method: The authors use a mathematical framework involving Jordan-Wigner and Fourier transformations to analyze the spectrum of a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian and calculate the dynamical and stationary Quantum Fisher Information (QFI).
model_or_system: A one-dimensional, long-range, RT-symmetric non-Hermitian iXY spin model with power-law decaying interactions, effectively modeled as a spin-1/2 lattice coupled to a reservoir.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for the transverse magnetic field (h) and anisotropy parameter (gamma), and scaling exponents (mu and beta).
important_parameters: Decay exponent (alpha), coordination number (Z), non-Hermiticity parameter (gamma), magnetic field (h), and the broken/unbroken phase regimes.
main_assumptions: Periodic boundary conditions are assumed, and the analysis focuses on a system initialized in a fully polarized state.
figures_summary: Figures show the ratio of non-Hermitian to Hermitian QFI versus alpha, the scaling of QFI with system size near exceptional points, and the lack of improvement in stationary scaling with increased coordination number.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the non-Hermitian long-range model, provides an analytical framework for its momentum-space decomposition, analyzes the metrological performance in both dynamical and stationary regimes, and concludes with a no-go result for stationary sensing.
why_interesting: It reveals a genuine metrological advantage arising from the synergy of non-unitary dynamics and long-range connectivity, providing new insights into how non-Hermitian many-body dynamics can be leveraged for enhanced quantum sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. QFI of the dynamical state for sensing magnetic fields in the long-range iXY model HiLR. (a) QFI FnH h (ordinate) vs time t (abscissa) for different coordination numbers Z at fall-off rate α = 1.5. (b) FnH h aganist t for different fall-off rates α with a fixed Z = 4. Both the insets show the scaling of QFI with time in the transient time t ≲2.0. Here, the imaginary anisotropy is taken to be γ = 0.3, h = −0.70 and the system-size is N = 1024. All axes are dimensionless.

FIG. 2. Scaling exponent ˜β (ordinate) of QFI, i.e., F nH h (t) ∼t ˜β in the transient time in the (a) broken and (b) unbroken regime against the coordination number Z (abscissa) for various fall-off rates α. The insets show the scaling exponent for the Z = N/2-case. Note that the long-range interactions increase ˜β with increasing coordination number and decreasing values of α. All other specifications are the same as in Fig. 1, and all axes are dimensionless.

FIG. 3. System-size scaling of QFI, given by Fnh h ∼N µ. (a) Fnh h (ordinate) with system-size N (abscissa) in the broken (h = −0.5) and the unbroken (h = −3.0) regimes, in the dynamics with long- range iXY model. The scaling exponent is higher in the broken regime with µ > 1, giving a non-Hermitian advantage in the long- range system. (b) Scaling exponent µ (ordinate) against the coor- dination number Z (abscissa) for various fall-off rates α. All other specifications are the same as in Fig. 1 and all axes are dimension- less.

FIG. 4. (a) Contourplot of scaling exponent ˜β of QFI in transient time in the (α, h)-plane for a fixed Z = N/2. (b) Map plot of system- size scaling exponent, µ, by varying fall-off rates α (abscissa) and magnetic field h (ordinate) with Z = 6. Here, in (a), N = 1024, t = 200 in (b) and in both (a) and (b), γ = 0.3. These plots indicate that the temporal and system-size scaling obtained from the dynamics is independent of phases. All axes are dimensionless.

FIG. 5. Time-average ratio of QFI in the non-Hermitian iXY model with QFI in the Hermitian XY model, defined in Eq. (12). Precisely, ⟨rnH h ⟩(ordinate) vs α (abscissa). In (a) h = −0.7, and (b) h = −1.50, which are in the broken and the unbroken regimes of the iXY model, respectively. Non-Hermitian models always provide benefits as the ratio is always above unity. In the broken regime (a), there are intermediate α values for which more precision can be obtained than the ones with low and high α values while in the unbroken regime (b), nonmonotonic but smooth behavior of ⟨rnH h ⟩ energes. Here the average of the ratio is taken from t = 200 to 1000 for a fixed system-size N = 1024. All axes...
